sentences.text: So then we-- they-- we were-- we were in that house, I would say, with almost at least about 100 other people in that house.
We were sleeping on the attic, and the basement, or so on.
Then one night-- one night I heard a crash like somebody fell down.
It was one of-- of my best friends from school, because he was in the attic too.
And of course, there is an opening where you go up and down in the attic.
And it was in middle of the night.
He didn't realize it.
He just stepped down in there.
They didn't even want to take him to the hospital.
Finally, they took him to the hospital.
But I found out later on, when they liquidated the ghetto, they took him from that hospital straight to Auschwitz.
Of course, he was never seen alive again.
Anyway, then they told us first to go to the train.
My sister Sarah what used to be with my aunt all the time, we ran-- I went over to her and told her she should come with us together.
She said, no, I'm not going to leave my aunt alone.
So we came to the train.
By the train, they told us to leave everything what we don't need, to leave there.
Of course, we didn't have much what we didn't need.
So we took everything with us.
They packed us in in that train.
There was hardly a place to breathe.
They put a couple buckets.
In other words, when you have to go, you should be able to go in those buckets over there.
Men, women, children, everything together.
We didn't know where we are going.
But as soon as the train started to go, we passed a few stations.
We realized we are going east.
We came-- of course, the guards on the train were Hungarians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Till we came to a station by the name of Kashau-- they used to call it Kosice.
Down there, the train stopped.
And I've seen the guards change.
My father saw it, too.
It came-- they became SS.
But before the Hungarians left, before the Hungarian guards left the train, they came out with plates.
And they said, all valuables-- gold, silver, diamonds, whatever you have-- please put it here, because you-- you wouldn't need them no more.
Those were the exact words what they said.
When my father saw the German SS guards, he started to cry.
He told me, it doesn't look good.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So we came.
We knew already we are going east, but where, we didn't know.
Till after a couple of days-- I think it was, I would say, about four days before [NON-ENGLISH], our holiday [NON-ENGLISH].
We came to a place.
We didn't know the name of it yet.
But as we came in and the train stopped, we saw-- they told us to go out of the train, to make a line, one after another-- women separate, men separate.
I don't have to describe the-- in the train, what was going on.
Do you know, in the train, when you're going for a few days, they don't give you no water, no food?
You don't have air.
You can't use a bathroom or where to use it, or what.
It-- I don't think that hell even is comparable to that.
Anyway, we came to that place where it's known now Auschwitz.
We had to make a line-- men separate, women with children separate, and so on.
They just opened up the-- the doors of the train.
Down there we saw already civilians.
But I mean civilians, people from the-- from Auschwitz, with-- with concentration camp uniforms on.
They were Jews, of course.
I would say it was around the middle of the day, yeah.
Then down there, we saw SS soldiers with dogs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And next to my mother, where she stood in the line there, my mother with my sisters-- with my sister-- yeah, sisters too, Golde and Chaia.
There was a neighbor.
She had twins.
She couldn't handle the twins.
They were young, about a year, a year and a half old.
So my mother grabbed one, one of the children.
And me, my father was in front of me.
I was back.
And my brother was in the back of me.
Then there was that SS man.
I think it was Mengele.
It looked like him.
He was standing there, like, with a small rod, pointing.
He pointed my father to go to the right.
But my father was born with a limp.
One leg was shorter with about an inch.
The minute he started to walk to the right, he tapped him on the shoulder, pointed him to the left.
That was the last time I saw my father, without saying goodbye or anything.
It was the last time.
Of course, my mother took that child on her arm.
I think that was a mistake of her side, because she went to the left.
Because anybody what had a child, a young child, whatever it is, went to the left.
My brother went with me, also the same.
We still don't know where-- didn't know where and what.
Of course, whatever we had yet, a little package, a suitcase, they told us to leave it there.
Some people said, but I need it.
They told them, we'll bring it to you later.
Then finally, we came to-- to a barrack.
We came there, they told us to shave complete.
The public place, the whole thing has to be shaved.
Everything has to be left there because you're going to get a shower.
We still didn't know what all-- what-- what's all about.
We got a-- they gave us a shower, true.
We shaved.
They gave us a shower.
We came out of the shower, they gave us clothes.
And they took us in a barrack, all of-- the whole group, all young people.
No older people, just young people.
At night, you smelled, you smelled that smell, like flesh.
You smell it.
Then we saw-- I saw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like two chimneys.
Basically, fire was going out.
And we asked one-- he was a Polish Jew.
He was in that-- in that barrack with us.
He was actually the orderly in that place.
We asked him, what is all that?
He says in Yiddish, he said, [YIDDISH].
They're burning Jews down there.
I-- we still couldn't believe it.
Still couldn't believe.
What do you mean-- what do you mean, they're burning Jews?
He says, [YIDDISH].
The food what we got there in Auschwitz was a piece of bread and some black coffee.
We were in Auschwitz-- I would say I was there about two days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, at the middle of the night, they woke us up, and they told us to go out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we went outside.
And there was that-- he was a German.
He was a kapo.
That's first time I knew what a kapo means.
He was a very heavyset guy.
And he says we should line up in five.
He said it in German, of course-- [GERMAN].
So my brother-- I wanted to be with my brother, but my brother was the fifth.
And I stood near my brother.
That means I was the sixth.
He came over.
He had a nightstick.
He whacked me over the head.
He split my head open.
[GERMAN].
In other words, he said, you damn Jew.
I said, five.
And my blood was-- was coming down.
It started to get daylight already.
As we marched toward the train-- now I know it was to the train.
As we marched to the train, we passed-- we passed a barrack where girls were outside.
All of a sudden, I heard somebody yells, Heshi!
That used to be my name.
Used to call me Heshi.
I see it's my sister, Chaia, shaven.
She used to have gorgeous hair-- all shaved.
She said in Yiddish, Heshi, [YIDDISH]?
That means, where is the mother?
I was so choked up I couldn't even-- even answer her.
I never saw my sister again.
That was the last word I heard from her.
Then they took us to the-- to the station.
We took-- we took the train.
This time, surprisingly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was passenger cars, not cattle cars no more.
They took us to Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Plaszow is near Krakow.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Plaszow was a labor camp.
It was a concentration camp, it was a labor camp.
They made-- that used to be the Jewish cemetery of Krakow.
They leveled the whole thing.
They leveled the cemetery.
They made a camp out of it.
In that camp itself, they had like small factories.
They had, let's say, tailor shops, where they made outfits for the soldiers, for the German soldiers.
They had a shoemaker's shop where they made shoes for the soldiers, and so on.
When we came down from the train, one guy what was already in that camp, a Yiddish fellow, says to me-- he said like this here, [YIDDISH].
In other words, say that you have a trade.
As I knew a little bit of shoemakers, I said, [GERMAN].
I'm a shoemaker.
My brother, he was young.
They put him as a-- what to clean-- what to clean the barrack where we were living.
Me, they assigned as a shoemaker in a shoe factory to make shoes for the German-- for the German soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The food-- The food consisted there of barley soup and a piece of bread.
There was on the other side== there were men and women, but they were separated with barbed wire.
Almost every day, every day I used to see somebody committing suicide.
He threw himself on the-- on the electric wires, on the electric current.
Almost every day, somebody did it.
The-- the guards, the German guards-- special the women that I have seen-- were much more brutal than the men guards in Plaszow.
One day, I've seen, they caught about three Jews in Krakow.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They lined them up.
They made them dig a trench.
They lined them up against the trench over there.
There was-- I'll never forget that picture of that person.
One guy had red hair.
When they put the machine gun in the front of them, and we-- they told-- and we had to watch that.
They put the machine gun right in front of him.
Basically, I could swear, his hair stood up from fright.
Of course, they shot them right there, those three people.
So till it came-- we existed in Plaszow.
We existed there.
We worked.
Till it came around August, we could hear already the Russian-- the Russian cannons.
The Russian front came already closer to Krakow.
They took us all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the-- on the place over there.
And they told us that-- that we are going to go to the train.
This is the first time I have seen-- down there, I've seen the first time guards, Lithuanians.
They were the most brutal ones you could find.
If you didn't run enough, they just-- with-- with-- with sticks, with nightsticks, they just hit you.
They just hit you over the head.
The Jewish kapos in Plaszow were not one of the pleasant ones, either.
I've seen one Jewish kapo beating one of our town people that it's-- it's humanly impossible to describe.
I'm not talking a German beating a Jew, that there.
That a Jew should beat one other Jew like that.
That was a Jewish kapo.
So anyway, we came to the train over there.
They packed us into the train.
There was, I would say, at least about 100-- 100-- 100 people in-- in a boxcar.
They sat us down first, let's say, one row.
Then the other rows had to sit between the legs for the other one, like this seat, to be able to pack in, to be able to pack in all of us in those trains.
Where we're going, we didn't know.
But they gave us-- they gave us a quarter-- I would say it was about-- about a quarter of a kilo, a piece of bread.
And we went in the train, and the train started to move.
It was hot.
Of course, they locked the door, but they left a little bit open by the doors.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were-- and we weren't so much hungry as we were thirsty.
For some reason or another, even God didn't have any mercy on us that time.
It was so hot.
It was impossible.
Maybe because we were too many people together, we felt more heat.
But the third day became-- we realized it's-- it's Austria.
I was sitting by the door opening.
I didn't have it so bad yet.
I was sitting by the door opening.
We came to a town in Austria.
I don't remember the name of that town.
The train stopped.
Then I heard the guards talking.
They were talking over there that they're going to-- they're going to leave some of us down, they're going to leave five people from every-- from every car to bring water for the people in the car.
Well, when they opened that door, I didn't wait.
I was down.
I just jumped down.
They gave me a bucket.
I don't know if you ever saw the-- with the locomotives, when they filled the locomotives with water, there is like a big-- a big crown what pours in the water of the locomotive.
They opened that crown.
We went under there.
I never drank so much water, I think, in all my life as I drank that time.
Then we started-- we started to feed, to give water for the other people.
There is another face I'll never forget, a Hungarian Jew.
You could see the white for his-- from thirst.
But he was begging, I should give him water.
He says to me, [NON-ENGLISH].
Give me a little water, I'm dying.
Took us about an hour to bring everybody water.
Then the train started to move.
We came to a-- to a place they call Mauthausen.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They marched us-- they marched us to the camp.
When we saw that-- the outside of that camp, we thought, oh, my god, we are in paradise here.
Beautiful-- beautiful flowers on the outside of the camp.
It-- it was really fantastic.
But when they opened the camp and we went in-- inside, in Mauthausen, the picture changed, like from day to night.
First, we had to get undressed.
All our clothes, we had to put in a site.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then there was one fellow, he was insisting to keep his pants.
I couldn't understand why.
But in a few minutes, I-- I understood why he did it.
One of his legs, his left leg was false.
The-- the kapo, he was also German.
He ripped off his pants.
And when he saw that, he just sent him in a side.
Of course, I know what-- what his end will be.
They checked everybody for diamonds, mostly.
They looked in places what it's not mentionable.
If anybody had any-- any jewelry, dimes-- I mean, diamonds, or anything like that.
They gave us a shower down there.
After the shower, they looked again.
Then they gave us uniform to put on.
They send us-- send us in a barrack also.
But here was an entirely different picture.
The beds, it was three.
There were three rows.
The beds, they were no mattress or anything on it.
There was just about four or five pieces of wood across.
Four to a bed.
That means 12 in a row.
The meantime, they didn't give us anything to eat yet.
They woke us up 4 o'clock in the morning.
They gave everybody an aluminum plate.
They poured in an aluminum plate coffee.
It looked like coffee.
It was black.
They give us five minutes to drink that coffee.
Now you put hot coffee, specially in aluminum, you cannot even put it to your lips.
Whoever didn't have it finished had to-- had to spill it out.
Then we had to run, I would say, about a mile.
Down there, as we came to the mile, there was what in German they call it [GERMAN].
That is-- how they call it in English already?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Well, I don't have the right name for it here.
In other words, it's a valley where they cut stones.
There was about 180 steps, or 183 steps go down.
A quarry is the English name.
Everybody had to take a stone to put it next to him and wait.
The SS man went by.
He looked at the stone.
He didn't like the size of the stone, he just told you to step aside.
If he liked the size of the stone, you put your stone on your shoulder, and you ran up those 183 steps.
That person what didn't-- what the stone didn't fit in the eyes for the SS man, you never saw him again.
We had to run with that stone for another mile, throw it down, run back again.
This is like this here till about 12 o'clock of the day.
At 12 o'clock, they gave us a soup.
The soup was-- I think it was-- it was cooked-- cooked cucumber.
That's what it was.
The same thing.
It was hot like fire.
The sun was burning.
And they told you just to eat it up, without a spoon, by the way.
Didn't give you no spoon or anything, just from the plate itself.
You didn't finish it, out it goes.
Again, running the same quarry, down, over again.
I think we started off-- we started off that time and became-- I think we started off, from Plaszow, about 300-- over 300 people in there.
After two weeks, we were about 24 left.
Then they took us again on a train.
We came to a city named Melk.
That's also in Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [GASP]
As we reached Melk, when we came down from the train, there were no SS waiting for us.
It was the Luftwaffe.
That means the--
The air force-- I'm sorry.
It means the air force.
We were very happy about it.
We came over there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they told us that we are going to work here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened in Melk-- what happened in Melk was this here.
They were building a factory in a mountain.
They were building-- I think it was-- it was ball bearings.
They-- they wanted to make it in the mountain, because-- for the-- for the American and the English Air Force shouldn't be able to bomb them.
So the next day, we went to work.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH].
My work was there, with an air hammer, to dig in the mountain the stone.
In other words, well, to make, like, a tunnel.
Then there was another crew.
They were working with cement.
OK, I'm sorry.
We have to end the tape here.
All right, thank you.
